source.title: Role of the basolateral amygdala in memory consolidation.

confidence_rationale: Highly cited mechanistic review synthesising decades of pharmacological evidence for BLA NE/ACh-mediated arousal-dependent hippocampal memory modulation. The arousal-triggered 2h NA/ACh elevation and beta-adrenergic-dependent LTP facilitation map directly to MECH-074a's encoding_gain mechanism. Source quality high; mapping fidelity good but gain quantification is inferred from pharmacology rather than measured directly as a scalar.
failure_signatures: Inverted-U shape is not directly tested; evidence is monotonic facilitation across moderate arousal range, not bidirectional attenuation at high arousal.
BLA effects on hippocampal plasticity are mediated indirectly via rhinal/entorhinal projections, not necessarily by direct BLA-CA1 synaptic contacts, complicating the gain-on-write-strength framing.

mapping.source_claim_statement: BLA NA and ACh concentrations are transiently elevated (~2h) after emotionally arousing learning; BLA lesions block stress hormone memory enhancement; post-learning BLA beta-adrenergic or muscarinic antagonists reduce retention. Theta-frequency rhythmic BLA activity during arousal may promote plasticity in co-active temporal lobe structures.
mapping.ree_translation: BLA arousal-dependent NE/ACh release is the biological substrate of MECH-074a's encoding_gain scalar: elevated arousal (z_harm_a magnitude) produces a time-limited boost in hippocampal write strength, implemented in REE as a multiplicative gain with an exponential post-event decay window.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The inverted-U gain profile (attenuation at very high arousal) is not directly demonstrated in this paper; Pare's evidence supports monotonic facilitation across the moderate arousal range tested. The 2h biological window maps loosely to REE's ~18000 sim-step post-event window.
mapping.source_context: Rodent and human pharmacological studies, inhibitory avoidance and fear conditioning paradigms.

confidence_components.notes: Well-replicated NE/ACh mechanism is highly conserved across rodent-human; gain quantification inferred not measured directly.
